passages.explanation: The Kingdom of Heaven will be like ten virgins who take their lamps and go out to meet the bridegroom. Five are foolish and five wise. The foolish take their lamps but no oil. The wise take oil in their vessels with their lamps. While the bridegroom delays, they all slumber and sleep. At midnight the cry goes out: the bridegroom is coming! Go out to meet him! They all rise and trim their lamps. The foolish say to the wise: give us some of your oil — our lamps are going out. The wise answer: there might not be enough for us and you. Go to the dealers and buy for yourselves. While they're gone, the bridegroom comes. Those who are ready go in with him to the wedding feast, and the door is shut. Later the others come: lord, lord, open to us! He answers: most certainly I tell you, I don't know you. Watch therefore, for you don't know the day or the hour. Readiness can't be borrowed at the last minute. Some things — faith, relationship, preparation — can only be your own.

It's like a man going into another country who calls his servants and entrusts his goods to them. To one he gives five talents, to another two, to another one — each according to his own ability. He who received five trades and makes five more. He who received two makes two more. He who received one digs in the ground and hides his lord's money. After a long time, the lord returns and settles accounts. The five-talent and two-talent servants both present their gains and hear the same words: well done, good and faithful servant. You were faithful over a few things — I'll set you over many. Enter into the joy of your lord. The one-talent servant says: lord, I knew you were a hard man, reaping where you didn't sow. I was afraid, so I hid your talent. The lord answers: you wicked and lazy servant! You should have at least put my money with the bankers, and I would have received my own with interest. Take the talent from him and give it to the one who has ten. To everyone who has, more will be given. From him who doesn't have, even what he has will be taken away. Cast the worthless servant into outer darkness. The parable isn't about investment strategy — it's about stewardship. The one-talent servant's real problem isn't his return rate; it's his distorted view of the master that produces fear instead of action.

When the Son of Man comes in his glory with all the holy angels, he'll sit on the throne of his glory. All the nations will be gathered before him, and he'll separate them as a shepherd separates sheep from goats — sheep on his right, goats on his left. The King will say to those on his right: come, blessed of my Father, inherit the Kingdom prepared for you from the foundation of the world. I was hungry and you gave me food. I was thirsty and you gave me drink. I was a stranger and you took me in. Naked and you clothed me. Sick and you visited me. In prison and you came to me. The righteous answer: Lord, when did we see you hungry, thirsty, a stranger, naked, sick, or in prison? The King answers: most certainly I tell you, inasmuch as you did it to one of the least of these my brothers, you did it to me. Then he says to those on the left: depart from me, you cursed, into the eternal fire prepared for the devil and his angels — for I was hungry and you didn't feed me, thirsty and you didn't give me drink, a stranger and you didn't take me in, naked and you didn't clothe me, sick and in prison and you didn't visit me. They protest: Lord, when? He answers: inasmuch as you didn't do it to one of the least of these, you didn't do it to me. These will go away into eternal punishment, but the righteous into eternal life. The final judgement turns not on theology but on compassion. Both groups are surprised — neither recognised Jesus in the faces of the vulnerable. The difference is what they did anyway.
